literal: non-knowledge; absence of *vidyā*; from a- (negative) + √vid 'to know'
invariant_definition: A condition of mis-taking that sustains bondage. In Sanskrit philosophical usage *avidyā* is rarely mere lack of information; it names the operative failure by which a subject mislocates self, object, and means of valid cognition. It therefore stands beside *vidyā*, presupposes a *pramāṇa* theory, and in Vedānta is tied to *adhyāsa* and the debate over error: Nyāya's *anyathā-khyāti*, Prābhākara *akhyāti*, Bhāṭṭa *viparīta-khyāti*, Yogācāra *ātma-khyāti*, and Advaita *anirvacanīya-khyāti*. Buddhist sources use *avidyā* as the first *nidāna* in dependent origination, while Yogācāra links its continuity to the latent dispositions carried in *ālaya-vijñāna*. Vedāntic schools disagree more radically: Advaita makes *avidyā* a beginningless positive factor with concealment and projection functions; Viśiṣṭādvaita treats bondage instead as contraction of the soul's cognitive reach caused by *karma*; Tattva-vāda (the school often labeled Dvaita) refuses any positive ignorance-substance and relocates the issue in real dependence, error, and divine governance. In practice the term names what must be removed, counteracted, or rendered non-operative by disciplined inquiry, right cognition, and, in the theistic schools, grace.

school_framing.shared_core: *Avidyā* names **the cognitive condition under which the subject misidentifies, omits, or obscures what would otherwise be manifest; the epistemic root of saṃsāric agency**. The shared structural role is the obstruction-of-vidyā. Schools differ on whether *avidyā* is *anirvacanīya* and *bhāvarūpa* (Advaita), merely the absence of right cognition (Madhva, Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā), the first link of *pratītyasamutpāda* (Buddhism), or *kleśa* among five (Yoga, YS 2.3).
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Real disagreement** (AF1) between Advaita's positive *bhāvarūpa* *avidyā* (Brahma-Siddhi 1.1; *Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa*) and Madhva's refutation of any *anirvacanīya* third category (*Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana*). **Different concept** in Buddhist *avidyā* (*pratītyasamutpāda*'s first link, not a positive *bhāva* on a substrate): the same *śabda* names different *artha* in Buddhist vs. Advaitic *pratyaya* (methodology §4).

per_school.definition: Advaita treats avidyā as anirvacanīyā rather than as mere abhāva, since error and vyavahāra require a positive explanatory factor and cannot be grounded in sheer non-being [[BS 1.1](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)] [[SB 1](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/1.prathamo vibhāgaḥ.1)] [[IṢ 1.1](cite://vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Prapañcasyānirvacanīyatvam)]. Within that register it is spoken of as jaḍātmikā avidyā-śakti and as ayathā-vastv-avidyā, the misapprehension whose nivṛtti is mokṣa through jñāna alone [[PañcP V.15–VI.16](cite://padmapada/pancapadika/V–VI.15–16)] [[NS 1.55-56](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.55-56)] [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)].
primary_loci: Siddhānta-Bindu, Prathamo Vibhāgaḥ, prose under verse 1

per_school.definition: Viśiṣṭādvaita refuses the thesis that Brahman itself is ignorant, deluded, or overrun by alien upādhis; that picture is treated as mohana lacking śruti- and nyāya-support [[VS §2](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§2)]. Accordingly, bondage belongs to the jīva's beginningless karmic entanglement and the resulting mistaken dehābhimāna, while Vedānta restores knowledge of the dehātirikta self and of Nārāyaṇa as antaryāmin through upāsanā grounded in tattva-jñāna [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)] [[VS §3](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3)].

Tattva-vāda denies that real bheda can arise from ajñāna: to make ignorance the cause of duality yields regress or mutual dependence, and no ajñāna can function as an āvaraṇa over what is already established [[UK 3](cite://madhva/upadhi-khandana/3)] [[MKh p. 238](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/can serve as a covering ignorance)] [[UK 15](cite://madhva/upadhi-khandana/15)]. The school instead places ajñāna, bodha, bandha, and āvṛti within the real order governed by Viṣṇu, so bondage is the dependent condition of the jīva within that order rather than the effect of an anirvacanīya ignorance-substance [[TS 10](cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/10)] [[TS 11](cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/11)].
primary_loci: Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, 'Nothing can serve as a covering ignorance'

per_school.definition: In Gauḍīya usage, the operative equivalent of avidyā is the jīva's māyā-mohitatva under Bhagavān's external śakti; the difference between Bhagavān and jīva is fixed precisely by his being māyāśraya and the jīva being māyā-mohita [[ParamS §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)]. Its undoing is therefore not the cancellation of relation but ceto-darpaṇa-mārjana through bhajana and nāma-saṅkīrtana, which extinguishes bhava and reorients the self toward service [[Śikṣ 1](cite://caitanya/shikshashtakam/1)] [[ParamS §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)].

For Nimbārka, the bound jīva is anādimāyā-pariyukta: a real aṇu-cit dependent on Hari yet enmeshed in beginningless māyā [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)] [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]. The practical force of avidyā is the continuing ajñāna-tamas removed through constant upāsanā of Kṛṣṇa [[DaŚ 6](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/6)].

Śuddhādvaita speaks of avidyā chiefly through its nivṛtti: brahmajñāna removes ignorance and restores the jīva's svarūpa-yogyatā for Puruṣottama-prāpti [[AṇuBh 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)]. That removal is not yet the whole of release, since bhakti under Bhagavān's choosing supplies the sahakāri-yogyatā by which attainment of Puruṣottama is consummated [[AṇuBh 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

In Sāṅkhya, the operative equivalent of `avidyā` is `viparyaya` or `ajñāna`: bondage is opposed to `tattvajñāna`, and the commentary on Kārikā 44 glosses this contrary cognition as the self-misidentification "I am admirable, I am the enjoyer," by which one binds oneself into embodied existence (cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/44, cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika-with-commentary/44--viparyaya-as-ajñana). The Sāṅkhya-Sūtra tradition makes the point polemically explicit by denying that bondage can arise from `avidyā` as a distinct non-real factor, so the school's explanatory burden falls on false identification and its removal by discriminative knowledge rather than on an `avidyā`-substance (cite://kapila/sankhya-sutra/1.20--na-avidyato-py-avastuna-bandhayogat).

Yoga places avidyā at the head of the kleśa-series that structures saṃsāric involvement; it belongs with asmitā, rāga, dveṣa, and abhiniveśa, whereas Īśvara as puruṣa-viśeṣa is untouched by the whole chain of kleśa-karma-vipāka-āśaya [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)]. Accordingly, avidyā is treated as a practical source of afflicted cognition to be neutralized within samādhi-sādhana, not as a separate world-projecting substance [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)].

Nyāya treats `avidyā` in the form of `mithyājñāna` or `moha`, not as the mere absence of knowledge: Vātsyāyana defines it as the erroneous grasp of self in what is not self, `anātmany ātmagrahaḥ`, and makes that error the `saṃsārabīja` whose removal requires `tattvajñāna` of the relevant objects (cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.1--mithyajnana-moha-anatmany-atmagrahah). Vaiśeṣika states the same register with greater brevity by classifying `avidyā` as defective cognition produced from `indriyadoṣa` and `saṃskāra`, while `vidyā` is the corresponding non-defective cognition (cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/9.25-27).

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā does not posit `avidyā` as an independent ontological principle; its functional analogue is `mithyājñāna`, a `pramāṇa`-failure identified through `bādha` and traced to defects in sense, mind, or object (cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5--mithyajnana-from-dosa). Śābara accordingly defines unsupported seeing as `mithyādarśana` or `vyāmoha`, exemplified by shell-silver error, so ignorance is handled through `pramāṇa`, `bādha`, and `doṣa` rather than through a world-projecting nescience (cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/3.8.24--mithyadarsana-vyamoha).

In Mādhyamaka, what functions as avidyā is svabhāva-grāha: the failure to see that no dharma possesses own-being either in itself or in dependence-relations [[MMK 1.3](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.3)]. Karma and kleśa arise from vikalpa and prapañca, and prapañca comes to rest only in śūnyatā; hence liberation is the cessation of proliferative misconstrual, taught through the two truths [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)] [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8)] [[MMK 24.10](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.10)].

Yogācāra locates the continuity of bondage in vijñāna-pariṇāma: imputations of self and dharmas arise within cognition, whose ālaya-form bears the sarvabīja continuity of maturation [[Triṃ 1](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/1)] [[Triṃ 2](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/2)]. Ignorance is thus operative as ātmamoha, as the grāhadvaya-vāsanā that renews karmic maturation, and as the anuśaya that endures until cognition is established in vijñaptimātratā [[Triṃ 6](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/6)] [[Triṃ 17](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/17)] [[Triṃ 19](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/19)] [[Triṃ 26](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/26)].

In Pratyabhijñā / Trika, `avidyā` is the `ajñāna-timira` by which the one self apprehends its own nature under the diversified polarity of `grāhya` and `grāhaka` (cite://abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/25). At the saṃsāric level it is explicitly the `lokavyavahāra`-producing `avidyā` that binds beings to birth and death through `dharma` and `adharma`, while liberation is defined as the cutting of the knot of `ajñāna` and the manifestation of one's own `śakti` (cite://abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/54, cite://abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/60).

Jainism handles the functional domain of `avidyā` through `viparyaya` and `mithyādarśana` rather than through a single metaphysical ignorance-principle: the Tattvārtha-Sūtra admits erroneous cognition in `mati`, `śruta`, and `avadhi`, and names false view, non-restraint, negligence, passions, and `yoga` as the causes of bondage (cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.31, cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.1). That bondage is concretized as karmic obscuration, above all the `jñānāvaraṇa` and `mohanīya` classes, so ignorance is treated as a morally efficacious distortion of cognition and orientation rather than as mere absence of knowledge (cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.4).

per_school.not_applicable_reason: `avidyā` is not a school-defining doctrinal category of the Pāṇinian-Vaiyākaraṇa tradition in the way that terms like `śabda`, `sphoṭa`, or `vākya` are, and the available corpus here contains no grammatical treatment requiring acquisition.

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'ignorance.' That English word suggests a merely psychological lack, as though one simply had not yet learned some fact. In these debates *avidyā* carries causal, epistemic, and soteriological weight: it structures misidentification, error, and bondage. One must also avoid importing Augustinian privation-talk. Advaita explicitly denies that *avidyā* is mere absence; Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda reject the Advaitin positive-entity account but still do not mean ordinary ignorance in the thin modern sense.
